speaker: interviewer
text: You're retired. And tell me about your family, your daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: What? Our daughter? There's lots to say. She-- she helped us with everything. My wife used to phone me up and tell you [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to prepare food for us, to clean the house. And she cut the grass. She shoveled the snow. She made the food. She help us from the university she come, she help us in the store. Not much in the store. In the restaurant. We used to have a restaurant. This kid did everything-- everything for us. Without her--

speaker: interviewer
text: When she got engaged, we figured out this our right arm. How are we going to survive without her? She was everything for us. She was very good. I mean, she was making babysitting money, things. Everything, she give it to us. And she help us in the business. She help us in-- in the home.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? WIFE: ?] She was [INAUDIBLE]. She come-- she come-- we come home, everything was done-- cooked, washed, cleaned, everything. And the only thing she begged us, when she's going to get a new-- a new dress. A new dress. But we treat her right. We saved our money. When she was 16, when she was 13, Bat Mitzvah, we couldn't have afford nothing for her. And she was 16, we give her a trip to Europe. She met her future husband. And when she come back, she told us all about it. And we bought her a sports car, SS sport car. She chosen. And on account, she can go to university. And we, that's what she liked, that she worked all your life, you saved your money. Our money we put in. We bought her for cash this car. And then she decided for your girlfriend to move to Toronto. And in university that year she was beautiful. The principal from university come into the store. And he ask us why she want to leave. She's so good, and they want her. She got a beautiful future. And I told him you're not going to keep our daughter, you know. That's what she want, that's what she get. Anyway, she took your car and your girlfriend, and they went to Toronto. They moved to Toronto. And she worked there. She learned there. Then one day she phone us up. She want to come with your boyfriend. He want to get engaged, if you like it. And she come with him here. And we told her-- you know, I told her if you want to get married, it's your life. You chose it. You got so many rich boyfriends. She said, Dad, you've got, too, if you want [? to. ?] You chose what you like.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter and son-in-law are now living in Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They live in Toronto. Anyways, when our daughter proposed, and we agreed to get married, and they arranged the wedding. But my wife and me, we decided just in Winnipeg on account I don't know why. The in-laws, and my-- the son-in-law, everybody wanted in Toronto where they got a very big family. And yes, we decided to, and they agree to have the wedding here. Until today, Rabbi Rapoport, when he sees me, he says up till today still didn't have a wedding like that. So beautiful wedding. Up till today, still didn't have any synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the whole family-- cousins, close relatives-- come. They flied in. They took two planes or one plane? Two planes come in. And they took a hotel, a whole hotel. And they were here a few days. And we made here the engagement party, at Westin Hotel, the wedding in the Rosh Pina.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they went back to Toronto. And they were both working. And we didn't like the idea of young kids in this age, they should be self-sufficient. So they told us the only thing to finish in engineer, and for her to finish a [INAUDIBLE] to go to Ottawa. And that costs lots of money. So we agreed to pay the bill, you know, buy another car for them, and to move to Ottawa, and to go in university there to finish their degrees [INAUDIBLE]. But it was very hard to get in there. A flight into Ottawa, I got the right connections, and they accept them, and we rented right away apartment for them, and bought another used car. Why they both went different courses. They need cars. And time to got when they finished, they moved into Toronto. My lawyer, my wife went to Toronto. And we bought them a house. Why the in-law want to buy them a condominium. And I says no way, condominium. I wanted to raise kids. And we're used to self-sufficient, having your own home. Anyways, my wife went. And she find a home where they like it, put down-payment. They live up till today there. He got a beautiful job for the government in research. And she got a beautiful job. But finally now they are self-employed already about 10 or 12 years. Engineering. Computers. Anything. And research for the government. Part time for them, plus in his own. And she quit her job and took care of it, his office, to run the business. And they're both working. They've got workers. And they're doing very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I've got two grandchildren-- Jesse and Daniel. And when they're born, we locked up our business. We went into the bris for both of them. We didn't care. We locked our business for a week. And then for their Bar Mitzvahs we did the same thing. We already were retired for both of them. And the only thing what I like it is the we can't call them son-in-law to other son. They call us Abba. They called my wife, mom, Eema. The grandsons call us Saba and Savta.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else we want? We got the biggest, the biggest, the happiest people in the world. You know, money can't buy that with the respect. You know, still a son, you know, is a mine. He got a father And his father used to ask him, I'm your father. He says, but there's two fathers-- my wife's father. [INAUDIBLE]. That's a beautiful ending.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And you know, the respect-- and that's what keep us alive you know. We-- in our-- in our age we both in good shape. Just that keep us alive. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add as to how you felt you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: As to how you felt you survived? Any particular story that you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: What it's like to survive. But I want to add on here, finally we agreed to buy a home. And comes Swan Lake Development for six years in [INAUDIBLE]. There's always-- there's fishing, hunting, gambling. I mean, excuse me, camping. I'm sorry, I'm not a gambler. Never gambled in my life. Camping. So everything fitting into-- the hospital there-- everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And our daughter and our son told us don't worry. You sell your house what you get, and we pay the balance. You know, what else can a father and a mother to ask? You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they signed. They-- I mean, we went. We signed a contract, and down-payment, everything. And that's-- we now we're going to be in another year with the kids. And we are the happiest parents. Now from the war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is I wouldn't believe myself if somebody told me these things. I'm proud of myself, what they give me. The Russians and the Polish governments gave me a chance to fight for my freedom, and to take revenge, you know. And a survivor like a Jew. Everybody knew, and all the armies what I was. In every states, they knew I'm Jewish. And this is the most important in my life. I never hidden, or never they ask me to hide my identity. And I was proud. And in Poland, they called me the army with [NON-ENGLISH]. That's Moses, Moses fate, you know. And in Russian, Boryski Gregorovich. They don't mention your second name. They go for your name, first name, and your father's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: But everybody knew in the passport. I mean, I got all my driving license, all my medals, all my certification-- everything is in my name. And Eizenstein Borys. And what can I ask more? And the respect. When I was-- I got very mad on account here people say where you got your medals? I went to the Polish council to Toronto with my daughter and my grandsons. And I told them the story what I'm telling you now. I brought them the papers. And I still didn't get a few medals. From the War Department I didn't get a shortage. So I find papers. And I says, OK, I got occasion. I got medal the people who wanted some, OK in the papers. Plus, I start to look. And I got three or four declarations from the War Department. I didn't get my medals and shortage. So my daughter made appointment to the general council. We went in there. I brought in my papers, my certification. So they salute me. They told my daughter and my-- when I come in myself to the general council, he says if I come with someone. I says yes, I brought in my daughter and my two grandsons. And they told me they want to see them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he invite me conference room. And he brought in drinks and some cookies. And he says he wanted to have a speech for my daughter, for my grand-- for my grandsons. And he got a speech for them. That was worth for me, you know, billions. My life-- I wouldn't trade it in for the speech he give it to my grandsons and to my daughter. Even my grandson, the older one, got a Bar Mitzah. So he mentioned that to the public. And we got on tape. You know what the general council said. For him, that's what his grandfather did in Second World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I want to say, and then they send me just two medals. One identity. And I got highest. I tell you why. Before invading Russia, before invading Russia four days, they called him up out to the general staff to Prague. And they told me two guys coming in from Moscow from the Kremlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they looking for a driver, just a Jewish driver. They don't want nobody there. Just a Jewish driver. You the only one in our unit. You're the only one available. You go and drive them. They're going come with a plane. We're going show you where. We take you there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there. And they took off on the plane, two boxes-- never forget-- vodka. And the rest in the common cylinders. All wore nice cylinder. You know, the French? They got cylinders. All were in black, civil clothes. Two nice gentleman, young gentlemen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they give me the hand. They told me their names. I don't know. I don't remember. I told them my name. And they told me I'm going with them three days. They want to see the whole front line-- Russia, you know, the line where they're going invaded. And they wanted to see what's going on. They want to see the ammunition, that I'm going with them for three days, They got food for me, too. They got everything with me ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went with them. And the first day was OK in the front line. They went to each unit. They check the cartouches, the armory, the units. I don't know. I was sitting in the Jeep. And then they told me to get up, and food, you know, and to go into the company's-- you know, company's restaurants that have food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nighttime, they took me out. You know, they gave me a place where to sleep in each unit we were. Next day, we went another outfit deep in Poland, after Prague. The Germans in aeroplanes. Two aeroplanes from both side you know, on the roadside, you know, started shooting us-- not just at me. You know, it was army there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyways I told them hide. They didn't know the first time in their life. And we grabbed them, and we went in. We went into the ditches, you know. And the Jeep fall over, you know, from the-- from the plane when it went in from the air. You understand? From the air. It's like.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got hit in my eye. In the right eye I still got a star-- I mean a scar. And the vodka fall over, everything. Anyways, they got hit. So I saved them. And I grab right away passed by another unit. I grab in a Jeep. I tell them, you know, who we are. And what officers from the Russian army, they allow me. They give the Jeep. They represent themself, who they are. And I put them back there, how much bottles I could. And we went back to the headquarters there, and there was first aide. You know, they give me a patch in my right eye. They patched them up. And we were three days. After a month, I don't know. In the war time we went in already to Germany. They called me out to [INAUDIBLE], and they told me to come in from Moscow. I got in two-- two medals in my name. And they say when you get the chance, you come to get them-- the secretary from the staff. And then I find out he was from Odessa, a Jewish lieutenant; and a woman, a secretary, a corporal, Polish. And they told me, you know, when I can come in, you know, in order to get my two medals. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I come in to get the two medals, they told me they give it to the chief was a woman-- Petrovska. Here, a chauffeur, they give it to him, instead to me. I want to blab. And they told me if you blab, they're going to get rid of you easy. She's the boss. And I didn't get them till today. That's the only thing what I didn't get at what was mine. Everything else, I got it. But mine I never get it. And now, when the council send me about a year ago, they send me from Warsaw. I got the papers. They send me just two medals, but one identity-- the same what I got already. And another one is from Berlin. But I got from Berlin from Stalin, from the Russian army. Now they send me another medal from the Polish army, a medal for Berlin. But the real McCoy, I didn't see it, and I didn't get it. Someone get it. And his name is Vasily [INAUDIBLE]. You can prove it you, black and white. He still survive us. And he got my medals, is Vasily [INAUDIBLE]. That's what they told me. And that's what I know. If they didn't tell me, I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll look at your-- the medals that you do have.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead? OK. That's for liberation of Israel, [NON-ENGLISH] Israel. That's Ben-Gurion. David Ben-Gurion gave it to me personally in the police station Karkur. This one, I got it from the Polish woman from Warsaw-- excuse me-- liberate Berlin. That's from Poland. Then I got one Russian army, Stalin, liberation of Berlin. All Baltics liberation. And here I got from Warsaw, liberation of Warsaw. I was there fighting for two days. And we got in. The Germans, for 70 kilometers we chased them. We learned them a good lesson there, you know. And all different kinds of medals. I got them two, four, six, seven, eight, and Israeli is nine. And two I didn't get it from Russia. They give it to Vasily [INAUDIBLE]. These medals, my medals, they give it to the driver from Petrovska, the General Petrovska. That's what they told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And maybe you can briefly tell the story about the woman you met in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman in Israel, I was in going off on a show, me my wife, and another officer with me, with his wife, from a show. And in 1952 and '52 or '53, they start to put up stores, [NON-ENGLISH] in Israel. And we went to see the stores, what they got, what they're selling. And I was looking. We were laughing, what, look, we got in clothing, and, you know, pots and pans and shoes. And I was screaming, you know. I was so excited. And I called my wife. I said [INAUDIBLE]. Finally, start to move people, a woman a short woman with two kids in front of me. And I scared, maybe they want to kill you. We were in full uniform, you know, with weapons. And she run and she kissed. And she fall down, and she started to kiss my foot. And she said, that's the boy [SOB] what you liberate us. I says I recognize. And he says, I've [? gotten ?] your voice. You were screaming not to grab food. And thanks to you, we survived. I never forget your voice. I'm in a kibbutz so-and-so between-- between [NON-ENGLISH]. I got the name from the kibbutz. Anyway, she said we go and see you. And I told her I live in [NON-ENGLISH] in the police station. And after a few days, they come in a delegation kibbutz, and invite us to the kibbutz. They got a big Oneg Shabbat party for me, and for my wife, and from our daughter. And we promise them we going to come steady to be in contact. But my job never allow me to be in contact anymore. I never saw him anymore. That's the picture from my mom, Yocheved Eizenstein. The picture took in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Ludwipol?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ludwipol. Yes. In Ludwipol, Selisht. [CHUCKLES] OK. This declaration is they call me up, in the general staff in-- in Bratslaw. And then they send me back to Katowice. And they give it to me, the declaration from the war, where everywhere I was took part, in the fighting for freedom, for freedom Poland and Germany. From Warsaw, I took part up to Berlin. And here you've got proof. Everything is there, what we liberated, our unit.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] partisans from the hospital. And this is a group of pictures of friends in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep. This is a group of pictures from the partisans in Italy. What we were in a hidden place in Italy-- a very secret place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have a friend from Ludwipol that you'd like to identity.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the only one what I remember his name. That's from my own town from Ludwipol, [INAUDIBLE]. He was a real partisan and a real survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he is--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the real McCoy.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Real McCoy.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them, but [INAUDIBLE] was the real McCoy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's in the picture by himself as the formal portrait.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he's still alive in Israel up till today. He's the only one who survived. They got one daughter and two-- three grandchildren. His wife died-- Luba.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the pictures were taken in Italy between 1946 and 1948.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that, yeah, yeah, yeah. For passports.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are pictures that were taken around the time of your surgery in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Italy. In 1948-- the beginning of '48. And when I was in the hospital after the surgery, professor [INAUDIBLE] in the countryside-- I can't tell you the secret place, operated me with Agana.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they insist why he was a fascist. And he was scared. So they send up from Agana a doctor, one doctor woman from the Jewish Congress in Milan. And two guys from Agana was assistance in the surgery.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this prince were in my family. That he was a prince, like. He give us his villa to do our work. He rented that for us without money. He give it to us. And all assistance what we needed, what you can't buy for a billion, for no money, they did it a group. He met me with a dozen Italian, and they-- they give us everything what we need and what we wanted underground. And very dangerous things we did. And they risked their lives. And I don't know, they got in love with me. And-- and he give me 60 cubic blood transfusion. I have to get that emergency. It was no time for me to waste in the surgery after the x-rays. And professor [INAUDIBLE] kept me in his own observation personally. After three days, he feed me. He give me a smoke. And I have to be recovered for six months. And he send me to Lago Maggiore in his own private villa. And they took care of me for six months in Lago Maggiore. That's Swiss border. OK. On the left side, my picture. That was in the fighting uniform, when we went to fight. And the right side, after the war, I got it to picture in Katowice when I was working in general staff. And that was my uniform officially.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the Polish army.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish army, both of them, yeah. On the left, that's the first uniform from [NON-ENGLISH] Israel in 1948. And then that was with my friend. I was there in hospital in [NON-ENGLISH], Scotch hospital, branch six, [NON-ENGLISH], six. That was Scotch hospital in [NON-ENGLISH]. I was there about six months. And in the right side, that's the uniform from Israel, what the Ben-Gurion himself give us the hats, the sweaters in [NON-ENGLISH]. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of your buddy in the picture? [INAUDIBLE] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? OK, these I got pictures from the police force, when I was in 1971 in a conference, you know, together. And all for my background, for my being a officer, I got all kind of pictures more or less, a few pictures what I saved. And I got a few more. But that's the most important. What I wanted to-- to show. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just talk briefly about it , then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead? OK. I got the pictures for myself from my friends. Then I got a picture with my Jeep and [NON-ENGLISH] with a mukhtar from [NON-ENGLISH]. And that was given to us in 1949 from the UN. They call them [NON-ENGLISH]-- villages from Arab territories. That's the picture, 1951, when we took over Karkur, Mishteret Karkur by the border-- Jordanian border. And my wife was then pregnant in the last. She was nine month pregnant. Thank you. OK. This is a family picture. We took it here in Winnipeg, Studio 7. And that's me-- myself, Borys; Edna-- my wife; our daughter Yocheved and her husband Irvin, and the godsons-- grandsons Daniel and Jesse. That's my birth certificate-- metryka they call them in Polish. In 1938 I got that from Korets. And I kept it all the war from 1938 till 1947, '48. I kept it in my shoe-- in my shoes. I transfer from shoes to shoes. And I kept it all the wars, all the misery up till today. And I'm proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: You kept it inside your shoe? Or in the heel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the heel.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the heel.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the pictures from my wife's parents. Took them before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name is Hannah, and [NON-ENGLISH]. Your father's name is [NON-ENGLISH], and the mother's name is Hannah [? Dimmerman. ?] They are from Kostopil.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just say Kostopil, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kostopil, Poland. They are born in Kostopil, Poland.
